system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.

Pattern 41 - Cmaj7, Am7, or F#m7b5 (Cmaj7 Sub)

Pattern 41 is a simple major arpeggio run with a typical bebop rhythm.
This pattern is heard a lot in jazz guitar solos because it sits so well on the fretboard.

This Cmaj7 arpeggio can be used over Cmaj7, Am7, or F#m7b5.

Pattern 45 -— G7 (Harmonic Minor)

Similar to pattern 14, pattern 45 uses an harmonic minor scale over a dominant chord.

While the dominant in pattern 14 is going to a minor chord, you can also use the harmonic minor
scale on dominants going to a major chord.

You can play the tonic harmonic minor scale over any dominant chord.
For example: C harmonic minor over G7

When doing so, you create a 7b9, b13 sound over that chord.

Another name for the harmonic minor scale played over a dominant chord is the Phrygian dominant
scale.

| C harmonic minor = G Phrygian dominant

Pattern 49 -— G7 (Diminished Sub)

Diminished arpeggios are a common way to play over dominant chord and produce a 7b9 sound.

Playing a diminished arpeggio from the b9, 3, 5, or b7 of a dominant chord brings out the b9
sound.
For example: Abdim7 over G7

“an erpsasie A Slee

Soa

The convenient thing about diminished chords and arpeggios is that their inversions are identical in
shape and notes.

This is because the interval structure of diminished chords is symmetrical.

If you have a look at the intervals of a Cdim7 below, you will notice that the interval between each
chord note is a minor third.

minor 3rd minor 3rd minor 3rd minor 3rd

Pome ee ew Lanne

This means that you can use the same arpeggio shape for all inversions.


In the exercise below, I start the diminished arpeggio on a different chord note each time.

While the arpeggio shape stays identical, the way to resolve into the Cmaj7 changes with every
inversion, so it’s a good idea to get these under your fingers.

Starting on the b9 of G7
